attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
1	# actor-x1
2	
3	An experiment in creating an actor model in rust
4	see [notes/design.md](notes/design.md) for details.
5	
6	This is the main repo of a dual-repo convention for using
7	a bot to help in the development of a coding project. The goal
8	is that this main repo contains the "what", while the partner
9	bot repo contains "why" and "how". The key to the convention
10	is each change is cross-referenced to the other. Thus there
11	is a coherent story of the development of the project across time.
12	
13	The beginnings of that tool is [vc-x1](https://github.com/winksaville/vc-x1)
14	which currently does achieve this goal, but is being used as a
15	first test bed.
16	
17	## Cloning
18	
19	The Bot session repo is setup as a git submodule which
20	makes it easier to clone both repos. The easiest is to
21	clone both when doing the initial clone:
22	```
23	git clone --recurisve git@github.com:winksaville/vc-template-x1
24	```
25	
26	If you forget to use --recurisze you need to do two additional
27	steps "init" and "update":
28	```
29	git clone git@github.com:winksaville/vc-template-x1
30	git submodule init
31	git submodule update
32	```
33	
34	And these can be combined using 'update --init' if you like
35	```
36	git clone git@github.com:winksaville/vc-template-x1
37	git submodule update --init
38	```
39	
40	## Usage
41	
42	Stage 1 (the PoC landed at 0.1.0) provides two binaries:
43	
44	- **`goal1`** — two actors on one thread, ping-ponging a
45	  message through a shared `VecDeque`.
46	- **`goal2`** — two actors on two threads, ping-ponging over
47	  `std::sync::mpsc` channels.
48	
49	Install with `cargo install --path crates/actor-x1`; both share
50	a common CLI shape:
51	
52	```
53	$ goal1 0.5 --warmup 0 --inner 1000 --pin 0
54	actor-x1 0.2.0
55	goal1: 102087000 messages in 0.500s (204.174 M msg/s, inner=1000)
56	  pinning: main → core 0
57	  apparatus: framing=21 tk (5.54 ns); per-event at inner=1000 = 0 tk (0.00 ns)
58	
59	  tprobe: goal1.dispatch [count=102,087]
60	              first     last    range        count mean
61	    min-p1        0 ns     0 ns     0 ns         0    0 ns
62	    p1-p10        0 ns     0 ns     0 ns         0    0 ns
63	    p10-p20       4 ns     4 ns     0 ns    32,684    4 ns
64	    p20-p30       0 ns     0 ns     0 ns         0    0 ns
65	    p30-p40       0 ns     0 ns     0 ns         0    0 ns
66	    p40-p50       5 ns     5 ns     0 ns    30,128    5 ns
67	    p50-p60       0 ns     0 ns     0 ns         0    0 ns
68	    p60-p70       0 ns     0 ns     0 ns         0    0 ns
69	    p70-p80       5 ns     5 ns     0 ns    34,218    5 ns
70	    p80-p90       0 ns     0 ns     0 ns         0    0 ns
71	    p90-p99       5 ns     7 ns     2 ns     3,971    6 ns
72	    p99-max       7 ns    34 ns    27 ns     1,086    9 ns
73	    mean                                              5 ns
74	    stdev                                             1 ns
75	    mean min-p99                                      5 ns
76	    stdev min-p99                                     0 ns
77	```
78	
79	### Flags
80	
81	- **`<duration_s>`** — positional, required; measurement window
82	  in seconds.
83	- **`--warmup <SECS>`** — default 10.0; run the same dispatch
84	  loop with the probe active for this long, then clear the probe
85	  at the boundary so only steady-state data reaches the report.
86	  Set to 0 for quick iteration.
87	- **`--inner <N>`** *(goal1 only)* — default 1, ≥1; dispatches
88	  per probe scope. Larger values amortize the probe's two-`rdtsc`
89	  apparatus cost and give the histogram meaningful dynamic range.
90	  goal2 fixes `inner=1` because its per-channel queue holds at
91	  most one message.
92	- **`-t` / `--ticks`** — display probe values as raw ticks
93	  instead of nanoseconds.
94	- **`--raw`** — skip apparatus-overhead calibration; show the raw
95	  per-event cost including framing contamination.
96	- **`--pin <CORES>`** — pin threads to logical CPUs. goal1 uses
97	  the first core in the list; goal2 pins actor `i` to
98	  `cores[i % len]`. Accepts comma lists and ranges: `--pin 5`,
99	  `--pin 0,1`, `--pin 0-3`, `--pin 0,0` (oversubscribe).
100	
101	### Typical invocations
102	
103	```
104	# Short iterative smoke, no warmup:
105	goal1 0.1 --warmup 0 --inner 1000
106	
107	# Full measurement with pinning:
108	goal1 2 --inner 1000 --pin 0
109	
110	# Goal2 with actors on separate cores (same complex):
111	goal2 1 --pin 0,1
112	```
113	
114	## Reading the band table
115	
116	The `tprobe` block is a 12-row percentile histogram with four
117	summary rows. Every row renders every run, even when a band
118	holds no data — a zero row is distinguishable from a missing row.
119	
120	### Header
121	
122	```
123	tprobe: <name> [count=N]
124	```
125	
126	`<name>` identifies the probe (`goal1.dispatch`,
127	`goal2.dispatch.actor0`, …). `count=N` is the number of **scopes**
128	drained into the histogram, not total dispatches — at `--inner
129	1000` one scope covers 1,000 dispatches, so `count` here is
130	`(total_messages / inner)`. Total dispatch count is in the summary
131	line above.
132	
133	### Rows
134	
135	Twelve percentile bands in order: `min-p1`, `p1-p10`, …, `p90-p99`,
136	`p99-max`. Each row covers the samples whose rank in the sorted
137	distribution falls within that percentile range.
138	
139	### Columns
140	
141	- **first** — lowest value observed in the band.
142	- **last** — highest value observed in the band.
143	- **range** — `last - first + 1`; width of the band's value range.
144	- **count** — number of samples in the band.
145	- **mean** — average value within the band.
146	
147	### Summary rows
148	
149	- **mean / stdev** — whole-histogram statistics, including the
150	  `p99-max` tail.
151	- **mean min-p99 / stdev min-p99** — same statistics excluding
152	  `p99-max`. Useful for A/B-ing code changes because the tail can
153	  be dominated by rare outliers (OS preemption, lock contention,
154	  interrupts) that drown signal in the body of the distribution.
155	
156	### Why a band can be all zeros
157	
158	The renderer walks `hdrhistogram` buckets and assigns each bucket
159	to one band by its cumulative mid-rank. A tight distribution has
160	most samples in a handful of buckets, and each bucket lands
161	entirely in one band — so neighboring bands show zeros even
162	though they'd nominally hold ~10% of the data. It's a display
163	artifact, not missing data: the samples are visible in the
164	adjacent non-zero band. A `stdev min-p99` near 0 alongside a
165	couple of dense bands is a strong signal that the underlying
166	distribution is spike-heavy.
167	
168	### The `apparatus:` line
169	
170	```
171	apparatus: framing=21 tk (5.54 ns); per-event at inner=1000 = 0 tk (0.00 ns)
172	```
173	
174	Reports the fitted overhead model. `framing` is the hardware
175	floor cost of the probe's two-`rdtsc` pair, measured by a
176	two-point fit on empty `black_box(1)` scopes. `per-event at
177	inner=N` is what the report subtracts from each stored value —
178	`framing / N`, which amortizes toward zero as `inner` grows.
179	Under `--raw` this line reads `raw (no overhead subtraction)`
180	and calibration is skipped.
181	
182	### The `pinning:` line
183	
184	```
185	pinning: actor0→core0, actor1→core4
186	```
187	
188	Shows what `--pin` produced for each workload thread;
189	`none (unpinned)` means the OS is free to schedule threads
190	anywhere.
191	
192	## The bot's understanding
193	
194	A handful of recurring patterns in the output, with the bot's
195	best read of why the numbers look the way they do. Specific
196	numbers below are from an AMD Ryzen 3900X at ~3.7 GHz — compare
197	your own numbers to your own baseline, not to these.
198	
199	### goal1 `--inner 1` shows ~9 ns; `--inner 1000` shows ~5 ns
200	
201	The probe's two-`rdtsc` framing has a real cost (~4 ns on modern
202	x86) that contaminates every stored scope delta. At `inner=1`
203	the full framing lands on every sample, pushing the reported
204	per-event cost to ~9 ns; at `inner=1000` framing is amortized
205	(~0.004 ns per event), so the stored value converges on the
206	actual `handle_message` cost of ~5 ns. `--raw` makes this
207	concrete — `--raw --inner 1` reads ~9 ns, `--raw --inner 1000`
208	reads ~5 ns. Default overhead calibration subtracts the framing,
209	so adjusted `--inner 1` also lands at ~5 ns, matching
210	`--inner 1000`.
211	
212	### goal2 is ~900× slower than goal1 `--inner 1000`
213	
214	goal1 `--inner 1000`: ~5 ns per event (~200 M msg/s).
215	goal2 `--inner 1`: ~1–1.5 µs per event (~0.25 M msg/s).
216	The bot thinks the gap is dominated by:
217	
218	- **`mpsc`'s `Mutex` + condvar wake** on every send and recv —
219	  even a fast bounded mpsc pays this on every cross-thread
220	  hand-off.
221	- **Two context switches per round-trip** — one per channel
222	  hand-off, so a ping-pong cycle of (A send → B recv → B send →
223	  A recv) crosses the scheduler four times.
224	- **Cache-line bouncing** — the shared channel metadata and the
225	  message migrate between the two actors' L1 caches each round.
226	
227	An unbounded lock-free mpsc would close some of this; the
228	context-switch floor is fundamental to any "block, wake, run"
229	channel pattern.
230	
231	### `--pin` tightens `stdev` but can raise the mean
232	
233	Pinning eliminates OS thread-migration noise — a thread landing
234	on a different core than the previous message's cache
235	warmed — so `stdev min-p99` drops (on this box: 246 ns → 80 ns,
236	about 3× tighter). But pinning also *forces* placement, which
237	can be worse than what the OS would have chosen. On this 3900X
238	(12 cores split across 4 CCX complexes of 3 cores each),
239	`--pin 0,4` puts the two actors on different CCX complexes, so
240	every ping-pong round forces a cross-complex cache coherence
241	transfer — the mean rises from ~630 ns to ~1,500 ns. `--pin 0,1`
242	(same CCX) keeps the mean near unpinned while still tightening
243	the stdev.
244	
245	This isn't a flaw; it's the intended trade. Pinning gives you
246	control over what you measure — pick cores that match the
247	scenario you care about (best-case same-complex, worst-case
248	cross-socket, etc.).
249	
250	### The `p99-max` tail occasionally spikes to tens of µs or ms
251	
252	Almost always an OS interrupt, scheduler preemption, or a rare
253	lock contention sneaking into a probe scope. The probe sees real
254	events the system experiences, so the measurement is honest —
255	they just aren't what you typically want to compare across
256	runs. `mean min-p99` excludes this tail and is usually the
257	right comparator for A/B work.
258	
259	### Numbers travel with the hardware
260	
261	- **Framing** is ~20–50 ticks regardless of clock speed
262	  (pipeline cost in cycles, not time).
263	- **goal1 per-event cost at large `inner`** scales with clock
264	  frequency.
265	- **goal2 latency** depends heavily on CPU topology (single
266	  socket vs NUMA), cache architecture, and scheduler tuning.
267	
268	If the numbers on your box differ from those above, that's
269	expected; consistency run-to-run and sensitivity to
270	`--pin` / `--inner` / `--raw` are the invariants worth trusting.
271	
272	## jj Tips for Git Users
273	
274	See [Steve Klabnik](https://github.com/steveklabnik)
275	[Jujutsu-tutorial](https://steveklabnik.github.io/jujutsu-tutorial)
276	and [jj docs](https://docs.jj-vcs.dev/latest/).
277	
278	### Initial Commit for a repo
279	
280	Create create directory add files.
281	
282	Minimal commands to push 
283	
284	```
285	jj git init .
286	jj describe
287	jj git remote add origin git@github.com:winksaville/vc-template-x1
288	jj bookmark create main -r @
289	jj bookmark track main --remote=origin
290	jj git push
291	```
292	
293	### Push a change to main
294	
295	Assuming that this is to be push to main you
296	set the bookmark to the appropriate commit and
297	then just push:
298	
299	```
300	jj bookmark set main -r @
301	jj git push
302	```
303	
304	Complete example:
305	```
306	wink@3900x 26-03-13T17:26:21.177Z:~/data/prgs/rust/vc-template-x1 ((jj/keep/1a79f803025f75fb557a7b6f9d29e3dbee6a1724))
307	$ vi README.md 
308	wink@3900x 26-03-13T17:28:08.833Z:~/data/prgs/rust/vc-template-x1 ((jj/keep/1a79f803025f75fb557a7b6f9d29e3dbee6a1724))
309	$ jj log
310	@  vnsyoswv wink@saville.com 2026-03-13 10:28:15 main* 3ac24f49
311	│  feat: Update README.md
312	◆  vuwzvmwm wink@saville.com 2026-03-13 09:38:22 main@origin 1a79f803
313	│  feat: Initial commit for the vibe coding main repo
314	~
315	wink@3900x 26-03-13T17:28:15.704Z:~/data/prgs/rust/vc-template-x1 ((jj/keep/1a79f803025f75fb557a7b6f9d29e3dbee6a1724))
316	$ jj git push
317	Changes to push to origin:
318	  Move forward bookmark main from 1a79f803025f to 3ac24f49321b
319	git: Enumerating objects: 5, done.
320	git: Counting objects: 100% (5/5), done.
321	git: Delta compression using up to 24 threads
322	git: Compressing objects: 100% (3/3), done.
323	git: Writing objects: 100% (3/3), 790 bytes | 790.00 KiB/s, done.
324	git: Total 3 (delta 2), reused 0 (delta 0), pack-reused 0 (from 0)
325	remote: Resolving deltas: 100% (2/2), completed with 2 local objects.
326	Warning: The working-copy commit in workspace 'default' became immutable, so a new commit has been created on top of it.
327	Working copy  (@) now at: kywoutls c26d415e (empty) (no description set)
328	Parent commit (@-)      : vnsyoswv 3ac24f49 main | feat: Update README.md
329	wink@3900x 26-03-13T17:28:33.741Z:~/data/prgs/rust/vc-template-x1 ((main))
330	```
331	
332	### Example of modifying an existing commit and "force" push
333	
334	Tweak a commit and push it using `jj edit` then "force" push:
335	
336	Minimum steps changing xx but it could be any commit on main
337	or other bookmark/branch the last step repositions @ so @- is main:
338	
339	```
340	jj edit -r xxx --ignore-immutable
341	<Modify the commit such as, `jj describe or `vi README.md`>
342	jj git push --bookmark main
343	jj new main
344	```
345	
346	A complete example, the `jj log` commands are to just give
347	a little more visibility. The thing I'm changing is the conventaional
348	commit type for of vnsyoswv is "feat" is should be "docs":
349	```
350	wink@3900x 26-03-13T17:32:17.819Z:~/data/prgs/rust/vc-template-x1 ((jj/keep/1a79f803025f75fb557a7b6f9d29e3dbee6a1724))

424	wink@3900x 26-03-13T18:25:46.005Z:~/data/prgs/rust/vc-template-x1 ((main))
425	$
426	```
427	
428	### Why `jj log` shows fewer commits than `gitk`
429	
430	If you're coming from git, jj's log output can be surprising compared to
431	tools like `gitk --all`.
432	
433	jj tracks *changes* (identified by change IDs), not individual git commits.
434	When you rewrite a change (`jj describe`, `jj rebase`, `jj squash`, etc.),
435	jj creates a new git commit and keeps the old one under `refs/jj/keep/*` as
436	undo history. `gitk --all` sees all of these obsolete commits; `jj log` only
437	shows the current version of each change.
438	
439	### Useful commands
440	
441	| Command | Description |
442	|---------|-------------|
443	| `jj log` | Show recent visible commits (default revset) |
444	| `jj log -r ::@` | Show **all** ancestors of the working copy |
445	| `jj log -r 'all()'` | Show all non-hidden commits (needed if you have multiple heads/branches) |
446	| `jj st | Show the status of the Working and Parent commits |
447	| `jj st -r <chid> | Status of the commit, <chid> such as `@`, `@-`, `xyz` |
448	| `jj show | Show the Working commit, -r @ |
449	| `jj show -r <chid> | Show the commit, <chid> such as `@`, `@-`, `xyz` |
450	| `jj evolog -r <chid>` | Show the evolution history of a single change |
451	| `jj op log` | Show operation history (each rewrite operation) |
452	
453	
454	In a single-branch workflow, `jj log -r ::@` and `jj log -r 'all()'` give
455	the same result. Use `all()` when you have multiple branches or heads.
456	
457	## Cross-repo Linking with Git Trailers
458	
459	Commits in each repo use [git trailers](https://git-scm.com/docs/git-interpret-trailers)
460	to cross-reference their counterpart in the other repo. The `ochid`
461	(Other Change ID) trailer contains a workspace-root-relative path
462	and jj changeID:
463	
464	```
465	ochid: /.claude/xvzvruqo   # points to a .claude repo change
466	ochid: /wtpmottv            # points to an app repo change
467	```
468	
469	Paths always start with `/` (the workspace root, i.e. vc-x1).
470	Each repo has a `.vc-config.toml` that identifies its location
471	within the workspace, so tools can resolve these paths locally.
472	
473	For full details see:
474	- [Git trailer convention](./notes/chores-01.md#git-trailer-convention)
475	  — [ochid (Other Change ID)](./notes/chores-01.md#ochid-other-change-id)
476	  — [ChangeID path syntax](./notes/chores-01.md#changeid-path-syntax)
477	  — [.vc-config.toml](./notes/chores-01.md#vc-configtoml)
478	
479	## Contributing
480	
481	Bot-following workflow, commit conventions, and code style are
482	canonical in [CLAUDE.md](CLAUDE.md):
483	
484	- [Versioning during development](CLAUDE.md#versioning) — `-N`
485	  pre-release suffix convention (single-step vs multi-step).
486	- [Commit message style](CLAUDE.md#commit-message-style).
487	- [Commit-Push-Finalize Flow](CLAUDE.md#commit-push-finalize-flow) —
488	  two-checkpoint per-step discipline.
489	- [Code Conventions](CLAUDE.md#code-conventions) — doc comments on
490	  every file / fn / method, `// OK: …` on `unwrap*` calls,
491	  ask-on-ambiguity, stuck detection.
492	- [Pre-commit checklist](CLAUDE.md#pre-commit-checklist).
493	
494	Task tracking and release details live under [notes/](notes/):
495	near-term tasks in [notes/todo.md](notes/todo.md), per-release
496	details in `notes/chores-*.md`, and notes-specific formatting
497	rules in [notes/README.md](notes/README.md).
498	
499	## License
500	
501	Licensed under either of
502	
503	- Apache License, Version 2.0 ([LICENSE-APACHE](LICENSE-APACHE) or http://apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0)
504	- MIT license ([LICENSE-MIT](LICENSE-MIT) or http://opensource.org/licenses/MIT)
505	
506	### Contribution
507	
508	Unless you explicitly state otherwise, any contribution intentionally submitted
509	for inclusion in the work by you, as defined in the Apache-2.0 license, shall
510	be dual licensed as above, without any additional terms or conditions.
511	
512	[1]: https://github.com/karpathy/autoresearch
513

# actor-x1

An experiment in creating an actor model in rust
see [notes/design.md](notes/design.md) for details.

This is the main repo of a dual-repo convention for using
a bot to help in the development of a coding project. The goal
is that this main repo contains the "what", while the partner
bot repo contains "why" and "how". The key to the convention
is each change is cross-referenced to the other. Thus there
is a coherent story of the development of the project across time.

The beginnings of that tool is [vc-x1](https://github.com/winksaville/vc-x1)
which currently does achieve this goal, but is being used as a
first test bed.

## Cloning

The Bot session repo is setup as a git submodule which
makes it easier to clone both repos. The easiest is to
clone both when doing the initial clone:
```
git clone --recurisve git@github.com:winksaville/vc-template-x1
```

If you forget to use --recurisze you need to do two additional
steps "init" and "update":
```
git clone git@github.com:winksaville/vc-template-x1
git submodule init
git submodule update
```

And these can be combined using 'update --init' if you like
```
git clone git@github.com:winksaville/vc-template-x1
git submodule update --init
```

## Usage

Stage 1 (the PoC landed at 0.1.0) provides two binaries:

- **`goal1`** — two actors on one thread, ping-ponging a
  message through a shared `VecDeque`.
- **`goal2`** — two actors on two threads, ping-ponging over
  `std::sync::mpsc` channels.

Install with `cargo install --path crates/actor-x1`; both share
a common CLI shape:

```
$ goal1 0.5 --warmup 0 --inner 1000 --pin 0
actor-x1 0.2.0
goal1: 102087000 messages in 0.500s (204.174 M msg/s, inner=1000)
  pinning: main → core 0
  apparatus: framing=21 tk (5.54 ns); per-event at inner=1000 = 0 tk (0.00 ns)

  tprobe: goal1.dispatch [count=102,087]
              first     last    range        count mean
    min-p1        0 ns     0 ns     0 ns         0    0 ns
    p1-p10        0 ns     0 ns     0 ns         0    0 ns
    p10-p20       4 ns     4 ns     0 ns    32,684    4 ns
    p20-p30       0 ns     0 ns     0 ns         0    0 ns
    p30-p40       0 ns     0 ns     0 ns         0    0 ns
    p40-p50       5 ns     5 ns     0 ns    30,128    5 ns
    p50-p60       0 ns     0 ns     0 ns         0    0 ns
    p60-p70       0 ns     0 ns     0 ns         0    0 ns
    p70-p80       5 ns     5 ns     0 ns    34,218    5 ns
    p80-p90       0 ns     0 ns     0 ns         0    0 ns
    p90-p99       5 ns     7 ns     2 ns     3,971    6 ns
    p99-max       7 ns    34 ns    27 ns     1,086    9 ns
    mean                                              5 ns
    stdev                                             1 ns
    mean min-p99                                      5 ns
    stdev min-p99                                     0 ns
```

### Flags

- **`<duration_s>`** — positional, required; measurement window
  in seconds.
- **`--warmup <SECS>`** — default 10.0; run the same dispatch
  loop with the probe active for this long, then clear the probe
  at the boundary so only steady-state data reaches the report.
  Set to 0 for quick iteration.
- **`--inner <N>`** *(goal1 only)* — default 1, ≥1; dispatches
  per probe scope. Larger values amortize the probe's two-`rdtsc`
  apparatus cost and give the histogram meaningful dynamic range.
  goal2 fixes `inner=1` because its per-channel queue holds at
  most one message.
- **`-t` / `--ticks`** — display probe values as raw ticks
  instead of nanoseconds.
- **`--raw`** — skip apparatus-overhead calibration; show the raw
  per-event cost including framing contamination.
- **`--pin <CORES>`** — pin threads to logical CPUs. goal1 uses
  the first core in the list; goal2 pins actor `i` to
  `cores[i % len]`. Accepts comma lists and ranges: `--pin 5`,
  `--pin 0,1`, `--pin 0-3`, `--pin 0,0` (oversubscribe).

### Typical invocations

```
# Short iterative smoke, no warmup:
goal1 0.1 --warmup 0 --inner 1000

# Full measurement with pinning:
goal1 2 --inner 1000 --pin 0

# Goal2 with actors on separate cores (same complex):
goal2 1 --pin 0,1
```

## Reading the band table

The `tprobe` block is a 12-row percentile histogram with four
summary rows. Every row renders every run, even when a band
holds no data — a zero row is distinguishable from a missing row.

### Header

```
tprobe: <name> [count=N]
```

`<name>` identifies the probe (`goal1.dispatch`,
`goal2.dispatch.actor0`, …). `count=N` is the number of **scopes**
drained into the histogram, not total dispatches — at `--inner
1000` one scope covers 1,000 dispatches, so `count` here is
`(total_messages / inner)`. Total dispatch count is in the summary
line above.

### Rows

Twelve percentile bands in order: `min-p1`, `p1-p10`, …, `p90-p99`,
`p99-max`. Each row covers the samples whose rank in the sorted
distribution falls within that percentile range.

### Columns

- **first** — lowest value observed in the band.
- **last** — highest value observed in the band.
- **range** — `last - first + 1`; width of the band's value range.
- **count** — number of samples in the band.
- **mean** — average value within the band.

### Summary rows

- **mean / stdev** — whole-histogram statistics, including the
  `p99-max` tail.
- **mean min-p99 / stdev min-p99** — same statistics excluding
  `p99-max`. Useful for A/B-ing code changes because the tail can
  be dominated by rare outliers (OS preemption, lock contention,
  interrupts) that drown signal in the body of the distribution.

### Why a band can be all zeros

The renderer walks `hdrhistogram` buckets and assigns each bucket
to one band by its cumulative mid-rank. A tight distribution has
most samples in a handful of buckets, and each bucket lands
entirely in one band — so neighboring bands show zeros even
though they'd nominally hold ~10% of the data. It's a display
artifact, not missing data: the samples are visible in the
adjacent non-zero band. A `stdev min-p99` near 0 alongside a
couple of dense bands is a strong signal that the underlying
distribution is spike-heavy.

### The `apparatus:` line

```
apparatus: framing=21 tk (5.54 ns); per-event at inner=1000 = 0 tk (0.00 ns)
```

Reports the fitted overhead model. `framing` is the hardware
floor cost of the probe's two-`rdtsc` pair, measured by a
two-point fit on empty `black_box(1)` scopes. `per-event at
inner=N` is what the report subtracts from each stored value —
`framing / N`, which amortizes toward zero as `inner` grows.
Under `--raw` this line reads `raw (no overhead subtraction)`
and calibration is skipped.

### The `pinning:` line

```
pinning: actor0→core0, actor1→core4
```

Shows what `--pin` produced for each workload thread;
`none (unpinned)` means the OS is free to schedule threads
anywhere.

## The bot's understanding

A handful of recurring patterns in the output, with the bot's
best read of why the numbers look the way they do. Specific
numbers below are from an AMD Ryzen 3900X at ~3.7 GHz — compare
your own numbers to your own baseline, not to these.

### goal1 `--inner 1` shows ~9 ns; `--inner 1000` shows ~5 ns

The probe's two-`rdtsc` framing has a real cost (~4 ns on modern
x86) that contaminates every stored scope delta. At `inner=1`
the full framing lands on every sample, pushing the reported
per-event cost to ~9 ns; at `inner=1000` framing is amortized
(~0.004 ns per event), so the stored value converges on the
actual `handle_message` cost of ~5 ns. `--raw` makes this
concrete — `--raw --inner 1` reads ~9 ns, `--raw --inner 1000`
reads ~5 ns. Default overhead calibration subtracts the framing,
so adjusted `--inner 1` also lands at ~5 ns, matching
`--inner 1000`.

### goal2 is ~900× slower than goal1 `--inner 1000`

goal1 `--inner 1000`: ~5 ns per event (~200 M msg/s).
goal2 `--inner 1`: ~1–1.5 µs per event (~0.25 M msg/s).
The bot thinks the gap is dominated by:

- **`mpsc`'s `Mutex` + condvar wake** on every send and recv —
  even a fast bounded mpsc pays this on every cross-thread
  hand-off.
- **Two context switches per round-trip** — one per channel
  hand-off, so a ping-pong cycle of (A send → B recv → B send →
  A recv) crosses the scheduler four times.
- **Cache-line bouncing** — the shared channel metadata and the
  message migrate between the two actors' L1 caches each round.

An unbounded lock-free mpsc would close some of this; the
context-switch floor is fundamental to any "block, wake, run"
channel pattern.

### `--pin` tightens `stdev` but can raise the mean

Pinning eliminates OS thread-migration noise — a thread landing
on a different core than the previous message's cache
warmed — so `stdev min-p99` drops (on this box: 246 ns → 80 ns,
about 3× tighter). But pinning also *forces* placement, which
can be worse than what the OS would have chosen. On this 3900X
(12 cores split across 4 CCX complexes of 3 cores each),
`--pin 0,4` puts the two actors on different CCX complexes, so
every ping-pong round forces a cross-complex cache coherence
transfer — the mean rises from ~630 ns to ~1,500 ns. `--pin 0,1`
(same CCX) keeps the mean near unpinned while still tightening
the stdev.

This isn't a flaw; it's the intended trade. Pinning gives you
control over what you measure — pick cores that match the
scenario you care about (best-case same-complex, worst-case
cross-socket, etc.).

### The `p99-max` tail occasionally spikes to tens of µs or ms

Almost always an OS interrupt, scheduler preemption, or a rare
lock contention sneaking into a probe scope. The probe sees real
events the system experiences, so the measurement is honest —
they just aren't what you typically want to compare across
runs. `mean min-p99` excludes this tail and is usually the
right comparator for A/B work.

### Numbers travel with the hardware

- **Framing** is ~20–50 ticks regardless of clock speed
  (pipeline cost in cycles, not time).
- **goal1 per-event cost at large `inner`** scales with clock
  frequency.
- **goal2 latency** depends heavily on CPU topology (single
  socket vs NUMA), cache architecture, and scheduler tuning.

If the numbers on your box differ from those above, that's
expected; consistency run-to-run and sensitivity to
`--pin` / `--inner` / `--raw` are the invariants worth trusting.

## jj Tips for Git Users

See [Steve Klabnik](https://github.com/steveklabnik)
[Jujutsu-tutorial](https://steveklabnik.github.io/jujutsu-tutorial)
and [jj docs](https://docs.jj-vcs.dev/latest/).

### Initial Commit for a repo

Create create directory add files.

Minimal commands to push 

```
jj git init .
jj describe
jj git remote add origin git@github.com:winksaville/vc-template-x1
jj bookmark create main -r @
jj bookmark track main --remote=origin
jj git push
```

### Push a change to main

Assuming that this is to be push to main you
set the bookmark to the appropriate commit and
then just push:

```
jj bookmark set main -r @
jj git push
```

Complete example:
```
wink@3900x 26-03-13T17:26:21.177Z:~/data/prgs/rust/vc-template-x1 ((jj/keep/1a79f803025f75fb557a7b6f9d29e3dbee6a1724))
$ vi README.md 
wink@3900x 26-03-13T17:28:08.833Z:~/data/prgs/rust/vc-template-x1 ((jj/keep/1a79f803025f75fb557a7b6f9d29e3dbee6a1724))
$ jj log

wink@3900x 26-03-13T17:28:33.741Z:~/data/prgs/rust/vc-template-x1 ((main))
```

### Example of modifying an existing commit and "force" push

Tweak a commit and push it using `jj edit` then "force" push:

Minimum steps changing xx but it could be any commit on main
or other bookmark/branch the last step repositions @ so @- is main:

```
jj edit -r xxx --ignore-immutable
<Modify the commit such as, `jj describe or `vi README.md`>
jj git push --bookmark main
jj new main
```

A complete example, the `jj log` commands are to just give
a little more visibility. The thing I'm changing is the conventaional

```

### Why `jj log` shows fewer commits than `gitk`

If you're coming from git, jj's log output can be surprising compared to
tools like `gitk --all`.

jj tracks *changes* (identified by change IDs), not individual git commits.
When you rewrite a change (`jj describe`, `jj rebase`, `jj squash`, etc.),
jj creates a new git commit and keeps the old one under `refs/jj/keep/*` as
undo history. `gitk --all` sees all of these obsolete commits; `jj log` only
shows the current version of each change.

### Useful commands

| Command | Description |
|---------|-------------|
| `jj log` | Show recent visible commits (default revset) |
| `jj log -r ::@` | Show **all** ancestors of the working copy |
| `jj log -r 'all()'` | Show all non-hidden commits (needed if you have multiple heads/branches) |
| `jj st | Show the status of the Working and Parent commits |
| `jj st -r <chid> | Status of the commit, <chid> such as `@`, `@-`, `xyz` |
| `jj show | Show the Working commit, -r @ |
| `jj show -r <chid> | Show the commit, <chid> such as `@`, `@-`, `xyz` |
| `jj evolog -r <chid>` | Show the evolution history of a single change |
| `jj op log` | Show operation history (each rewrite operation) |


In a single-branch workflow, `jj log -r ::@` and `jj log -r 'all()'` give
the same result. Use `all()` when you have multiple branches or heads.

## Cross-repo Linking with Git Trailers

Commits in each repo use [git trailers](https://git-scm.com/docs/git-interpret-trailers)
to cross-reference their counterpart in the other repo. The `ochid`
(Other Change ID) trailer contains a workspace-root-relative path
and jj changeID:

```
ochid: /.claude/xvzvruqo   # points to a .claude repo change
ochid: /wtpmottv            # points to an app repo change
```

Paths always start with `/` (the workspace root, i.e. vc-x1).
Each repo has a `.vc-config.toml` that identifies its location
within the workspace, so tools can resolve these paths locally.

For full details see:
- [Git trailer convention](./notes/chores-01.md#git-trailer-convention)
  — [ochid (Other Change ID)](./notes/chores-01.md#ochid-other-change-id)
  — [ChangeID path syntax](./notes/chores-01.md#changeid-path-syntax)
  — [.vc-config.toml](./notes/chores-01.md#vc-configtoml)

## Contributing

Bot-following workflow, commit conventions, and code style are
canonical in [CLAUDE.md](CLAUDE.md):

- [Versioning during development](CLAUDE.md#versioning) — `-N`
  pre-release suffix convention (single-step vs multi-step).
- [Commit message style](CLAUDE.md#commit-message-style).
- [Commit-Push-Finalize Flow](CLAUDE.md#commit-push-finalize-flow) —
  two-checkpoint per-step discipline.
- [Code Conventions](CLAUDE.md#code-conventions) — doc comments on
  every file / fn / method, `// OK: …` on `unwrap*` calls,
  ask-on-ambiguity, stuck detection.
- [Pre-commit checklist](CLAUDE.md#pre-commit-checklist).

Task tracking and release details live under [notes/](notes/):
near-term tasks in [notes/todo.md](notes/todo.md), per-release
details in `notes/chores-*.md`, and notes-specific formatting
rules in [notes/README.md](notes/README.md).

- MIT license ([LICENSE-MIT](LICENSE-MIT) or http://opensource.org/licenses/MIT)

file.content:
# Notes

See [design.md](design.md)


## jj tips

For users new to jj see [jj-tips.md](jj-tips.md).

```
## Chores format

Filename: "chores-XX.md"
example: chores-01.md

Format of section labels: "## <short description> (X.Y.Z)"
example: "## Topic format description (0.1.0)"

Example chore file:
```
# Chores-01.md
 
General maintenance tasks and considerations for the project see other files for
more specific topics. A chore in a chores file provides quick information on the
how and why of a particular chore.

## Do something (1.3.1)

Describe something
```

## Versioning during development

This is using jujustiu, jj + git and we'll see how it goes. Below is my
git workflow, jj will be different but we'll have to discover that as
we go.

Every plan must start with a version bump. Choose the approach based on scope:

- **Single-step** (recommended for mechanical/focused changes): bump directly to
  `X.Y.Z`, implement in one commit. Simpler history.
- **Multi-step** (for exploratory/large changes): bump to `X.Y.Z-devN`, implement
  across multiple commits, final commit removes `-devN`.

The plan should recommend one approach and get user approval before starting.

For multi-step:
1. Bump version to `X.Y.Z-devN` with a plan and commit as a chore marker
2. Implement in one or more `-devN` commits (bump N as needed)
3. Final commit removes `-devN`, updates todo/chores — this is the "done" marker

The final release commit (without `-devN`) signals completion rather than amending
prior commits. This keeps the git history readable and makes it easy to see which
commits were exploratory vs final.

## Todo format

Todo.md contains two main sections "Todo" and "Done" each item is a
short explanations of a tasks and links to more details using 1 or more
references.

Multiple references must be separated: `[2],[3]` not `[2,3]` or `[2][3]`.
In markdown, `[2,3]` is a single ref key (won't resolve) and `[2][3]`
is parsed as display text `2` with ref key `3` (so `[2]` won't resolve).

Examples:

# Todo

- Add new feature X [details](chores-01.md#feature-x)
- Fix bug Y [1]

# Done

- Fixed issue Z [2],[3]

[1]: chores-01bugs.md#bug-y

file.content:
# actor-x1

## Overview

Actor-x1 is a system that uses Communicating
Sequential Process (CSP) invented by Tony Hoare
In 1978 and is commonly termed the actor model.

 - There will be a management system (MS) that:

   - Provides the capability to interact with
     the runtime providing control and to capture
     logs and assists in developing the app
   - Creates an hello world scaffolding
   - provides discovery capabilities of other
     other actors.

 - A runtime that drives the system and provides
   resources and services for the system
   - interfaces between the manager and the application
 
 - One or more actors running on one or more
   threads. Each actor adheres to these
   invariants:
   - Has on basic entry point; handler()
   - The handler() handles one message at a time and
     must run to completion as quickly as possible.
   - The handler() never ever blocks
   - If a handler() has tasks that take to much time
     a technique that can be used is to spawn
     threads to do work. That thread would then use
     messages to communicate with its parent and
     other actors as it does its work.

## Future capabilities
   - I would like Actors to be implemented as an HSM.
     This potentially allows an Actor to implement
     sophisticated algorithms and prove correct operation.

## Stage1 runtime

Design and implement the simplest runtime possible

### Message

struct Message {}

### Actor

Actor implements an Actor trait

trait Actor {
  // Handles a message 
  fn handle_message(msg: Message)

  // other methods..
}

### Goal1

- Run two actors on one thread.
- A single message which is an empty struct
- Message ping pongs between the two actors
  for duration in seconds specified as a f64
  in seconds from the command line

## Goal2

- Same as Goal1 but each actor on a separate thread.

## Stage2 runtime

We will design and implement the slightly more capable runtime

It supports 3 actors A, B and C

### Message

struct Message {
  src_id: u32,
  dst_id: u32,
  send_count: u64,
}

### Actor

Actor implements an Actor trait

trait Actor {
  // Create a new actor
  fn new(id: u32, name: &str) -> Self;

  // Return the actors name
  fn name(&Self) -> &str;

  // Handle a message
  fn handle_message(msg: mut Message);

  // other methods..
}

### Goal1

- Run 3 actors on one thread.
- Runtime "sends" 5 messages each to a random destination actor
- Each actor forwards the message they receive to a random
  dst_id that they select.

### Goal2
- Same as Goal1 but each actor on a separate thread.

**Current state**:
- On `main` at `c818b648` — `feat: workspace split complete (0.2.0)`.

- Workspace with two crates: `actor-x1` (binaries `goal1`/`goal2`) and `tprobe` (perf probe, just extracted through the `0.2.0-1…3` ladder).

**Conventions I'm tracking**:
- CLAUDE.md is the source of truth — no per-project memory dir.
- jj + `vc-x1` commit/push/finalize flow with two approval gates; run per step, stop dead after finalize.
- Conventional commits with `(X.Y.Z)` suffix; multi-step uses numeric `-N` suffix.
- Every `.rs` file gets `//!`, every fn/method gets `///`; `unwrap*` calls need `// OK: …`.
- "The bot thinks …" prefix for speculation in durable text.

**Open todos**: initial design, PoC manager implementation. Stage 2 runtime (3 actors w/ typed messages) is the next design horizon.

Ready for direction.

content.text:
Read the rendering and drain path. Current behavior: `TProbe::report` subtracts `ovh.per_event_ticks(batch)` from each `per_event_raw` *before* recording into the histogram, so every column today is already adjusted. Switching to the requested format requires storing raw values and computing `adj mean` at render time.

A few things I want to lock in before writing code:

1. **Name**: `adj mean` works; alternatives are `net mean` (net of overhead) or `ovh-sub mean`. Sticking with `adj mean` unless you prefer otherwise.

2. **Where adj applies in the table**:
   - Per-band rows: easy — `adj mean = raw_mean - correction`.
   - Summary rows — I'd render `adj mean` alongside `mean`, and `adj mean min-p99` alongside `mean min-p99`. `stdev` rows are shift-invariant, so no adj value there (blank in that column). OK?

3. **Correction per band**: correction is `framing / batch`. If the probe ever mixes batches, a per-band average correction is the right thing to subtract. In today's binaries batch is fixed per run so the average equals the constant. I'll track a per-band correction sum during drain so it's correct either way — trivial extra work. OK?

4. **`--raw` flag behavior**: today it skips calibration entirely. In the new format the `adj mean` column has nothing to show. I'd leave the column out of the header in that mode (rather than print blanks), and keep the `raw (no overhead subtraction)` apparatus line as-is. OK?

5. **Versioning**: single-step bump to `0.2.1` — mechanical display change plus test updates. Alternative is multi-step if you want the histogram-semantics change (raw vs adj storage) reviewed separately from the rendering change, but I think single-step is right here.

Confirm those and I'll draft the plan.

content.text:
Read the overhead module and the binaries' use of `--raw`. Two follow-ups:

**On #3 — why it's worth thinking about**

The correction is `framing / batch`, and `batch` is stored per-record. So:

- **If every record has the same batch** (today's PoC — `goal1` uses constant `inner`, `goal2` uses `batch=1`): correction is a single constant `c`, and `adj mean[band] = raw mean[band] - c`. Trivial.
- **If batches vary within one probe's records** (not used today, but possible later — e.g. an actor with mixed fast-path and slow-path scopes): records with small batches carry large corrections and records with large batches carry tiny ones. A single global `avg_correction` would under-correct the bands dominated by small-batch records and over-correct the others.

Two ways I can implement it:

  (a) **Global average correction.** Track total correction sum across the drain, divide by sample count, subtract uniformly. Exact when batch is constant (today); approximate otherwise. ~5 lines.

  (b) **Per-band correction sum.** During drain, track `band_correction_sum[i]` so each band's adj mean is accurate for its actual record mix. Exact always. Requires a little restructuring of the drain path (band assignment needs raw value, which we have, then a matching mid-rank walk after histogram is built).

Recommendation: (a) now. Leaves correct behavior for the PoC and a clean note marking where (b) kicks in if we ever mix batches. Agree?

**On #4 — what exactly does "at most 1/10 the duration" govern?**

Current calibration is fixed: ~10 ms total (`CAL_SAMPLES=1000` × two inner sizes). It does not scale with measurement duration.

Two readings of your ask:

  (i) **Hard cap only.** Keep calibration fixed at ~10 ms. If someone runs `goal1 0.05` (a 50 ms measurement), cap calibration at 5 ms so it doesn't dominate. No new flag.

  (ii) **Adaptive — bigger budget = tighter estimate.** Let calibration spend up to `duration / 10` by scaling `CAL_SAMPLES`. Long runs get more precise framing; short runs stay cheap. Optional flag `--cal-time <s>` overrides.

Which did you mean? I lean (ii) with the flag, but (i) is simpler if precision is already good enough.

Next action: you answer two open design questions — per-band correction strategy (a global-average vs per-band) and calibration duration policy (hard-cap vs adaptive with optional flag). (disable recaps in /config)

message.content:
#3 Define "framing" I assume it's "overhead" i.e. all the time outside of the "work" being measured, but I want to be sure.

#4, I see the issue, the default warmup is 10 seconds I ran `goal1 1` and it took a looooog time, I thought it was calibration, wrong it's warmup! That default is to long, I want to be able to quickly iterate intially and not have to specify warmup each time.

content.text:
**On #3 — framing is narrower than "all non-work time"**

Framing is specifically the hardware cost of the two `rdtsc` reads that bracket each scope: the `start_tsc = rdtsc(); ... ; end_tsc = rdtsc()` pair adds a fixed floor cost (~20 ticks / ~5 ns on x86) to every recorded delta, even when the work between them is zero. It's the probe's own apparatus cost — the bit the user can't remove by writing better work code.

Things that are NOT in framing:

- **`loop_per_iter_ticks`** — the calibration's own inner `black_box(1)` loop cost. Measured but *not* subtracted, because the user's real inner-loop cost (queue pop, deadline check, dispatch) *is* the thing they want to see. Subtracting a generic `black_box(1)` cost would distort that signal.
- **Other "non-work" time** — context switches, cache misses, branch mispredicts inside the scope, anything the OS or hardware throws in. Those are real things the user's code experiences, so they stay in the measurement.

So framing = "the cost of measuring, not the cost of everything outside the measured work". The comment-worthy distinction is apparatus-vs-environment, and we only subtract apparatus.

Updated "why (a) over (b)" stays the same: with batch constant per run today, global average is exact; when batches mix, per-band is more faithful.

**On #4 — warmup default**

Got it, different problem. The 10s warmup default is in the CLI (`--warmup`, default `10.0`). Calibration itself is ~10 ms and unrelated.

Proposal: drop default to something iteration-friendly. Two shapes:

  (i) **Fixed small default** — `--warmup 0.5` (or `0.25`). `goal1 1` becomes ~1.5 s total. Users who want real warmup pass `--warmup 10`. Simple.

  (ii) **Proportional** — default to e.g. `duration / 10` capped at `2.0` s. `goal1 1` → 0.1 s warmup; `goal1 10` → 1 s; `goal1 60` → 2 s. Scales naturally.

I lean (i) with `0.5` — predictable, one number to remember, explicit `--warmup 10` for measurement runs. Agree, or prefer (ii) / a different number?

Also — does the scope of this release now mean:
- New adj-mean format (+ raw histogram storage)
- Remove `--raw` flag, always calibrate
- Lower warmup default

…all in a single 0.2.1 commit? Or split the warmup default into its own tiny 0.2.2?

message.content: Lets and "notes/*" to each crate as eventually they are likely to be separated. Define framing in the docs and point to it in the code. Also, it seems to me we should be subtracing all "overhead" costs not just "framing", if possible.

content.text:
Three things to settle.

**Defining framing (and overhead) so we're precise**

These are the two things calibration actually produces:

- **Framing** (`framing_ticks`, per-scope): the cost of one `rdtsc` pair — the `start_tsc = rdtsc()` + `end_tsc = rdtsc()` bracket — plus whatever hardware bias that pair produces. Paid once per scope, independent of batch. Current calibration attributes the `y`-intercept of the two-point fit to framing.
- **Loop-per-iter** (`loop_per_iter_ticks`, per inner iteration): the slope of the same fit — the cost of one `black_box(1)` iteration in the calibration's empty inner loop. Paid once per iteration inside a scope.

Both are in the `Overhead` struct. Today we subtract only framing.

**"Subtract all overhead" — what's possible**

Three categories, by whether we can separately measure them:

1. **Framing** — measurable, unambiguous apparatus cost. Subtract. (Already doing this.)
2. **Loop-per-iter** — measurable, but it's the cost of the *calibration's* empty loop. The real dispatch loop has its own per-iter scaffolding (loop branch, counter increment) that's similar to `black_box(1)`, so subtracting this is a close-enough proxy — biased slightly low because the real loop doesn't contain the `black_box` itself. The earlier note "don't subtract, it's part of what the user measures" was cautious but arguably over-cautious: loop scaffolding *is* overhead relative to the dispatch work.
3. **Everything else** — cache misses, branch mispredicts, context switches, interrupts inside the scope. Not separately measurable; they're part of what the real system experiences and should stay in the number.

So "all overhead" in this codebase really means **framing + loop_per_iter**. I recommend we expand subtraction to both, document the slight-bias caveat, and keep the split visible in the `apparatus:` line so the reader can see what was removed. Agree?

**Per-crate `notes/` directories**

Proposal:

- `/notes/` (workspace — stays): `README.md` (format conventions), `chores-01.md` (release ladder spanning both crates), `todo.md`, `done.md`, `jj-tips.md`, `vc-notes.md`, `acronyms-terms.md`.
- `/crates/actor-x1/notes/`: `design.md` moves here (it's the actor-model design, not workspace-level).
- `/crates/tprobe/notes/`: new `design.md` describing the probe architecture, and new `overhead-model.md` that formally defines framing, loop-per-iter, and the subtraction policy. The code in `overhead.rs` / `tprobe.rs` gets `See crates/tprobe/notes/overhead-model.md` references replacing the long inline prose.

**Scope — do we still single-step?**

The ask has grown well past a display tweak:

1. notes reorg + `overhead-model.md` with framing definition
2. extend subtraction to framing + loop_per_iter
3. band-table format (raw cols + `adj mean`)
4. remove `--raw`, always calibrate
5. lower warmup default

I'd split into a multi-step `0.3.0` ladder:

- `0.3.0-0` — plan marker
- `0.3.0-1` — notes reorg + `overhead-model.md` (docs only, no code behavior change)
- `0.3.0-2` — extend subtraction to framing + loop_per_iter (one-line probe change + tests + apparatus-line update)
- `0.3.0-3` — band-table adj-mean column (TProbe stores raw, render gets correction)
- `0.3.0-4` — remove `--raw`, always calibrate
- `0.3.0-5` — warmup default → 0.5s
- `0.3.0` — close ladder; README update

Each step reviewable on its own; none are large. Agree, or prefer to compress any?

content.text:
Plan queued. Summary of the 0.3.0 ladder:

- **0.3.0-0** — version bump to `0.3.0-0`, chores-01 entry, todo.md `In Progress`.
- **0.3.0-1** — notes reorg: workspace `/notes/` keeps format/VCS/ladder content; each crate gets a `notes/` dir and `README.md`; move actor-model `design.md` into `crates/actor-x1/notes/`; write `crates/tprobe/notes/overhead-model.md` formally defining framing (per-scope rdtsc-pair cost), loop-per-iter (empty-loop slope), and the unmeasurable "everything else" bucket (cache misses, mispredicts, interrupts, context switches — they stay in the measurement because we cannot attribute them per-event). Code doc-comments shorten to `See crates/tprobe/notes/overhead-model.md`.
- **0.3.0-2** — `Overhead::per_event_ticks` returns `framing/batch + loop_per_iter_ticks`. `apparatus:` line shows both components so readers see the split. Tests updated.
- **0.3.0-3** — TProbe stores raw; band-table render takes correction; existing columns show raw; new rightmost `adj mean` column; summary rows `mean` and `mean min-p99` get adj values; `stdev` rows leave adj column blank (shift-invariant). Global average correction (exact for constant-batch PoC).
- **0.3.0-4** — drop `--raw` from goal1/goal2; always calibrate.
- **0.3.0-5** — `--warmup` default `10.0 → 0.5`.
- **0.3.0** — close the ladder; top-level README updated for new format, no `--raw`, new warmup default, per-crate READMEs.

Each step passes the pre-commit checklist and gets its own `vc-x1 push` with two approval gates per CLAUDE.md. After each finalize I stop and wait.

Ready to start **0.3.0-0** on your go.

23	  (ns by default, `-t/--ticks` for raw ticks). The bot thinks promoting
24	  this to a shared crate is worthwhile once Stage 2 is underway, but
25	  copying in now avoids a cross-repo dependency for the PoC.
26	- `0.1.0-3` — Goal2: same workload as Goal1 but each actor on its own
27	  thread, communicating via `std::sync::mpsc` channels. Shutdown by
28	  dropping senders. Probe instrumented the same way (per-thread
29	  `TProbe2`, report on join).
30	- `0.1.0` — final: drop the `-N` suffix, update `notes/todo.md` and
31	  `notes/README.md`.
32	
33	### Design decisions recorded here
34	
35	- **Actor trait signature** deviates from the design sketch by adding
36	  `&mut self` and a `&mut dyn Context` parameter, where `Context::send(dst_id, msg)`
37	  is the only way for a handler to emit messages. Required because
38	  the empty `Message {}` carries no reply-to information and the
39	  sketched signature `fn handle_message(msg: Message)` has nowhere
40	  to thread outbound sends through.
41	- **Two `Context` implementations**: the single-threaded runtime's
42	  context writes to a `VecDeque`; the multi-threaded runtime's
43	  context holds `Sender<Message>` per destination. Actors see
44	  only `&mut dyn Context` and are agnostic to which runtime is
45	  driving them.
46	- **x86_64-only for now**: `tprobe2`'s tick counter uses `rdtsc`
47	  and `iiac-perf/src/ticks.rs` emits a `compile_error!` on other
48	  arches. Development target is x86_64 so this is acceptable; the
49	  restriction is documented at the vendored module.
50	
51	## Goal1: single-thread ping-pong runtime (0.1.0-1)
52	
53	Implements Goal1 of Stage 1: two actors, one thread, ping-pong an
54	empty [`Message`] for a caller-supplied duration in seconds, then
55	report the message count and throughput.
56	
57	- `src/lib.rs`: `Message` unit struct, `Actor` trait
58	  (`handle_message(&mut self, &mut dyn Context, Message)`),
59	  `Context` trait (`send(dst_id: u32, msg: Message)`), and
60	  `pub mod runtime`.
61	- `src/runtime.rs`: `SingleThreadRuntime` owns a
62	  `Vec<Box<dyn Actor>>` and a `VecDeque<(u32, Message)>`; `run_for`
63	  pops a message, field-split-borrows `actors` and `queue`,
64	  constructs a private `SingleCtx` wrapping the queue, dispatches,
65	  counts. Terminates on deadline or queue drain. Unit tests cover
66	  seed drain, bounded ping-pong count (11 = seed + 5·2), and
67	  sequential id assignment.
68	- `src/bin/goal1.rs`: CLI takes one positional `<duration_secs>`
69	  f64; constructs two `PingPongActor`s (each replies once per
70	  received message to its peer), seeds one message into actor 0,
71	  runs, prints `goal1: <count> messages in <secs>s (<M msg/s>)`.
72	
73	Smoke run on this box: 0.5 s → ~19.3 M messages → ~38.7 M msg/s.
74	
75	### Deviation from the design sketch
76	
77	`struct Message {}` in `design.md` is realised as a unit struct
78	`pub struct Message;` — equivalent externally, instantiates as
79	`Message` rather than `Message {}`. Matches clippy's preference for
80	empty types; trivial to convert back if literal match matters.
81

131	## Inner batching, warmup, clap (0.1.0-3)
132	
133	Adds CLI polish and measurement-quality improvements to Goal1.
134	Scope:
135	
136	- **Inner batching** — one probe scope now covers `--inner N`
137	  consecutive dispatches (default 1). At larger `N` the probe's
138	  two-`rdtsc`-plus-push apparatus cost is amortized, and stored
139	  tick values land in a range where the histogram's 0.1 %-relative
140	  buckets actually resolve jitter.
141	- **Warmup** — `--warmup SECS` (default **10.0**) runs the same
142	  dispatch loop with the probe active, then clears probe state;
143	  measurement phase inherits stable-state CPU freq / cache /
144	  branch-predictor conditions. Warmup message counts are never
145	  reported.
146	- **clap** — hand-rolled arg parsing replaced with a derive-based
147	  `Cli`. Validates non-negative finite `duration_s` and `warmup`;
148	  enforces `inner ≥ 1`. Auto-generated `--help` / `--version`.
149	- **TProbe2 additions** (vendored-code divergence from iiac-perf):
150	  - `Record` gains a `batch: u64` field.
151	  - `TProbe2::end_batch(id, n)` appends with `batch = n`; existing
152	    `end(id)` sets `batch = 1`.
153	  - `TProbe2::report` now divides stored deltas by `batch` at
154	    drain time so the histogram always holds per-event cost,
155	    regardless of scope granularity.
156	  - `TProbe2::clear()` resets records buffer and histogram;
157	    called between warmup and measurement.
158	
159	File-level edits:
160	
161	- `Cargo.toml`: bump to 0.1.0-3; add
162	  `clap = { version = "4", features = ["derive", "wrap_help"] }`.
163	- `src/perf/tprobe2.rs`: `Record.batch`, `end_batch`, `clear`,
164	  per-event division in `report`. Doc comment at file head lists
165	  divergences from upstream. Three new tests
166	  (`end_batch_stores_batch_size`, `report_divides_batched_delta`,
167	  `clear_empties_records_and_hist`).
168	- `src/runtime.rs`: `run_for(duration, inner)` wraps each batch
169	  of `inner` dispatches in a probe scope; site_id is the first
170	  `dst_id` in the batch. Partial trailing batches (queue empties
171	  mid-scope) are discarded — scope dropped, count not incremented
172	  — so throughput numbers reflect whole-batch work only. New test
173	  `partial_trailing_batch_is_dropped` covers the drop path.
174	- `src/bin/goal1.rs`: clap-derive `Cli` with `duration_s`,
175	  `--warmup`, `--inner`, `-t/--ticks`. Runs warmup, clears probe,
176	  runs measurement, prints count/throughput (now also reports
177	  `inner=N`), then calls `probe.report`.

184	| `0.5 --inner 1000` | 205 M msg/s | 5 ns | ~10× throughput; per-dispatch mean drops because apparatus is amortized |
185	| `0.5 --inner 1000 -t` | 204 M msg/s | 18 tk | Ticks view; tail p99-max 449 tk vs ~115 k tk at inner=1 (batch smooths OS interrupts) |

195	## Always render all 12 percentile bands (0.1.0-4)
196	
197	Previously the renderer skipped any band where no hdrhistogram
198	bucket was assigned by mid-rank (`if band_count[i] == 0 { continue;
199	}`), so tight distributions produced reports with only 3–5 visible
200	rows and it was hard to tell at a glance whether a band was
201	genuinely empty or just not rendered. This step always emits all
202	12 rows (`min-p1` through `p99-max`); empty bands render as zeros
203	in every numeric column.
204	
205	File-level edits:
206	
207	- `Cargo.toml`: bump to 0.1.0-4.
208	- `src/perf/band_table.rs`: drop the skip-on-empty guard; the band
209	  loop now computes `(first_v, last_v, range_v, mean_v)` from
210	  either the band aggregates (for non-empty bands) or `(0, 0, 0, 0)`
211	  for empty ones. `unwrap_or(0)` `// OK: …` justifications updated
212	  ("rows always contains n_bands=12 entries").
213	
214	### Display example (inner=10, 0.5 s)
215	
216	All 12 rows always visible — the zero-valued bands make the
217	distribution's shape explicit:
218	
219	```
220	    min-p1        4 ns        4 ns        0 ns        8,712     4 ns
221	    p1-p10        0 ns        0 ns        0 ns            0     0 ns
222	    ...
223	    p40-p50       5 ns        5 ns        0 ns    4,794,952     5 ns
224	    ...
225	    p99-max       7 ns    3,197 ns    3,190 ns       61,924    10 ns
226	```
227	
228	### Caveat — this only fixes visibility, not the bucket-into-band
229	mapping
230	
231	The renderer still walks hdrhistogram buckets and assigns each
232	bucket to exactly one band by cumulative mid-rank. In the inner=10
233	smoke above, 4,794,952 samples (~89 %) land in `p40-p50` while
234	`p1-p10` through `p30-p40` stay at zero — the single hot bucket at
235	"5 ns" got attributed to one band, not spread across the percentile
236	range its samples actually span. The bot thinks the principled
237	fix (switch to `hist.value_at_quantile(q)` per band boundary) is
238	worth considering if the zero-band display starts feeling
239	misleading; deferred for now.

281	## Apparatus overhead calibration (0.1.0-6)
282	
283	Adds a calibration phase between warmup and measurement that fits
284	the probe's framing cost (the bias on every stored
285	`end_tsc − start_tsc` delta) and subtracts the appropriate per-
286	event correction at report time. `--raw` opts out.
287	
288	### Why two-point (not single-point)
289	
290	`rdtsc` isn't a serializing instruction, so two back-to-back reads
291	with nothing between can execute out-of-order and measure ~0 ticks
292	— below the true apparatus cost. `_mm_lfence` bracketing drains
293	the pipeline harder than real work does (measured 38 tk on this
294	box, 2× the real bias). The two-point fit gives the right answer
295	by varying `inner` on an empty bench and solving
296	`raw_delta = framing + inner · loop_per_iter` in `f64` (no
297	integer-division loss at small values).
298	
299	On this box the fit lands on **framing = 21 tk (~5.5 ns)**,
300	reproducible across runs. With the correction applied,
301	`inner=1` probe mean drops from 9 ns (raw) to 4 ns, which matches
302	the `inner=1000` raw mean of 5 ns (where the correction is
303	negligible) — both phases converge on the same actual
304	`handle_message` cost.
305	
306	### File-level edits
307	
308	- `Cargo.toml`: bump to 0.1.0-6.
309	- `src/perf/overhead.rs`: new module. Constants `CAL_WARMUP = 10_000`,
310	  `CAL_SAMPLES = 1_000`, `N_LOW = 100`, `N_HIGH = 10_000`. `Overhead`
311	  struct carries `framing_ticks`, `loop_per_iter_ticks`,
312	  `cal_raw_low_ticks`, `cal_raw_high_ticks`, `cal_duration`.
313	  `calibrate()` runs the fit; `Overhead::per_event_ticks(batch)`
314	  returns `framing_ticks / batch.max(1)` for subtraction. Two unit
315	  tests (amortization math + calibration smoke).
316	- `src/perf/mod.rs`: `pub mod overhead` + re-export
317	  `Overhead`/`calibrate`.
318	- `src/perf/tprobe2.rs`: `TProbe2::report` signature gains
319	  `overhead: Option<&Overhead>`; drain loop subtracts
320	  `ovh.per_event_ticks(batch)` from each `per_event_raw` before
321	  histogram insertion (via `saturating_sub`, clamp at 1). New
322	  test `report_subtracts_overhead`. Old tests updated to pass `None`.
323	- `src/bin/goal1.rs`: new `--raw` flag; calibrates on the warmed
324	  system between `probe.clear()` and the measurement `run_for`;
325	  prints an `apparatus: …` diagnostic line before the band-table
326	  report.
327	
328	### What's *not* subtracted
329	
330	Only `framing_ticks`. `loop_per_iter_ticks` (from the black_box
331	empty bench) is kept on `Overhead` for diagnostic visibility but
332	deliberately not subtracted — the real dispatch loop's per-iter
333	cost (queue pop, deadline check, SingleCtx construction, dynamic
334	dispatch) is part of what the user wants to measure, and
335	`black_box(1)`'s loop_per_iter doesn't model it anyway. User
336	already signed off on this scope.
337	
338	### Smoke
339	
340	- `goal1 0.5 --warmup 0 --inner 1` — 19.9 M msg/s, apparatus
341	  21 tk / 5.54 ns, probe mean min-p99 **4 ns** (from 9 ns raw).
342	- `goal1 0.5 --warmup 0 --inner 1000` — 204 M msg/s, apparatus
343	  correction 0 tk (rounds down), probe mean min-p99 5 ns
344	  (unchanged — raw already clean at this batch).
345	- `goal1 0.5 --warmup 0 --inner 1 --raw` — apparatus: "raw (no
346	  overhead subtraction)"; no calibration run.
347	- 14/14 tests pass. Calibration adds ~10 ms to default runs.

349	## Goal2: per-actor threads + mpsc channels (0.1.0-7)
350	
351	Implements Goal2 of Stage 1: two actors running on their own
352	threads, ping-ponging an empty [`Message`] over `std::sync::mpsc`
353	channels, with the same warmup/measurement/calibration lifecycle
354	as goal1.
355	
356	### Design decisions
357	
358	- **Factory closures instead of pre-boxed actors.** `add_actor`
359	  takes `FnOnce() -> A` where `A: Actor + Send + 'static`; the
360	  factory runs inside the actor's own thread. Keeps the [`Actor`]
361	  trait free of a `Send` bound and matches how real actor systems
362	  typically spawn state.
363	- **In-band shutdown via internal `Signal` enum.** Channel payload
364	  is `Signal { User(Message), ClearProbe, Shutdown }`, not
365	  `Message` directly. Main thread injects `ClearProbe` at the
366	  warmup → measurement boundary and `Shutdown` after measurement;
367	  actors match on the variant in their `recv()` loop. Avoids an
368	  out-of-band atomic flag and the associated polling.
369	- **`inner` fixed at 1.** Ping-pong keeps at most one message in
370	  flight per channel, so batched probe scopes (`inner > 1`) could
371	  never fill — every scope would hit the partial-batch drop path.
372	  The `--inner` flag from goal1 is not exposed; the hardcoded
373	  `inner=1` is reflected in the throughput line.
374	- **Per-actor probes, merged reporting.** Each actor thread owns
375	  a [`TProbe2`] named `"goal2.dispatch.actor{id}"`. Main collects
376	  `(count, probe)` on join and prints a per-actor band table (not
377	  merged — two independent distributions are more informative
378	  than one blended histogram in a ping-pong workload where the
379	  actors should be near-identical).
380	- **Calibration pre-spawn on main thread.** Matches goal1's
381	  calibration code path. Same CPU → same `Overhead` applies to
382	  all actor threads. Could be refined later to calibrate
383	  per-thread, but unnecessary for Stage 1.
384	
385	### File-level edits
386	
387	- `Cargo.toml`: bump to 0.1.0-7.
388	- `src/runtime.rs`: add `MultiThreadRuntime`, `Signal` enum,
389	  `ActorFactory` alias, `MultiCtx`, and `actor_loop` helper below
390	  the existing single-thread code. New `#[cfg(test)] mod mt_tests`
391	  with a ping-pong smoke test (50 ms warmup + 50 ms measurement).
392	- `src/bin/goal2.rs`: new binary mirroring goal1's CLI shape
393	  (version banner, help width cap, --warmup, -t/--ticks, --raw),
394	  no --inner. Constructs `MultiThreadRuntime`, adds two
395	  `PingPongActor` factories, seeds one message, calls
396	  `rt.run(warmup, measurement)`, prints summary + apparatus +
397	  per-actor reports.
398	
399	### Smoke (release, `--warmup 0`)
400	
401	- `goal2 0.5` — 116,495 messages in 0.5 s (~0.23 M msg/s).
402	  Mean per-dispatch ~1.17 µs; median ~1.34 µs; p99 tail to
403	  ~22 µs (mostly scheduling jitter). Actors near-symmetric
404	  (58,247 vs 58,248 messages).
405	- `goal2 -h` — wraps at 80 cols, shows the `actor-x1 0.1.0-7`
406	  banner, no `--inner` flag.
407	- `goal2 0.2 --raw` — apparatus: `raw (no overhead subtraction)`;
408	  calibration skipped.
409	- goal1 unchanged: still ~200 M msg/s at `--inner 1000`.
410	- Tests: 15/15 pass (adds `multi_thread_runs_measures_and_shuts_down_cleanly`).
411	
412	### The ~250× single-thread gap
413	
414	Goal1 at `inner=1000` hits ~205 M msg/s (~5 ns/dispatch);
415	goal2 at `inner=1` hits ~0.23 M msg/s (~4 µs/round-trip, ~1.2 µs
416	per-event inside the probe scope). The bot thinks the gap is
417	dominated by: (1) mpsc's `Mutex` + condvar wake for every
418	message, (2) two cross-thread context switches per ping-pong
419	round, (3) cache-line bouncing between the two actors' threads.
420	An unbounded channel or a lock-free mpsc implementation would
421	close some of this but the context-switch floor is fundamental.
422	
423	## `--pin` for thread CPU affinity (0.1.0-8)
424	
425	Adds `--pin <CORES>` to both binaries. Tightens the stdev of
426	probe reports by eliminating OS thread-migration noise, at the
427	cost of exposing placement sensitivity in goal2 (pinning to
428	cross-CCX cores raises the mean because every message forces a
429	cross-complex cache coherence round-trip).
430	
431	Adapted from `../iiac-perf/src/pin.rs` with only the two
432	primitives we need (`parse_cores`, `pin_current`); the affinity
433	save/restore, `--no-pin-cal`, and mask-summary helpers are
434	elided — Stage 1 doesn't need them.
435

439	- `src/perf/pin.rs`: new module. `parse_cores(&str) -> Result<Vec<usize>, String>`
440	  (comma-separated / range list, duplicates preserved for
441	  oversubscription) and `pin_current(Option<usize>)` (pin the
442	  calling thread; no-op on `None`). 7 unit tests for the parser
443	  (plain list, range, mixed, duplicates, empty string, reverse
444	  range errors, garbage errors).
445	- `src/perf/mod.rs`: `pub mod pin`.
446	- `src/runtime.rs`: `MultiThreadRuntime::run` gains a
447	  `pin_cores: &[usize]` parameter; actor `i` pins to
448	  `pin_cores[i % pin_cores.len()]` inside its spawned thread
449	  (before the actor factory runs or the probe is created). Empty
450	  slice = unpinned (existing behavior). The lone mt_test updated
451	  to pass `&[]`.
452	- `src/bin/goal1.rs`: `--pin <CORES>` flag (`Option<String>`;
453	  parsed via `pin::parse_cores` in `main`). Uses the first core
454	  of the list (goal1 is single-threaded); extra cores silently
